C01.name: Hallucination Instance
C01.definition: Model output contains a claim not entailed by provided context
C01.example: Participant described model fabricating legal citations

C02.name: Threshold Calibration Challenge
C02.definition: Difficulty determining acceptable metric value for domain
C02.example: Participant described ad hoc threshold setting process

C03.name: Explanation Opacity
C03.definition: Inability to provide interpretable reasoning for model output
C03.example: Participant described audit trail existing but unusable

C04.name: Prompt Template Brittleness
C04.example: Participant described accuracy drop after minor wording change

C05.name: Existing Tool Inadequacy
C05.definition: Current QA tooling insufficient for LLM-specific failure modes
C05.example: Participant stated unit tests pass but hallucinations go undetected

C06.name: Ad Hoc Quality Assessment
C06.definition: Quality evaluation performed without systematic framework
C06.example: Team relies on manual spot-checks and user complaints

C07.name: Metric Proliferation
C07.definition: Excessive number of metrics without clear priority or aggregation
C07.example: Participant listed 15+ metrics tracked with no composite score

C08.name: Benchmark Mismatch
C08.definition: Standard benchmarks do not reflect production quality concerns
C08.example: High HELM scores but poor user satisfaction in production

C09.name: RAG Attribution Failure
C09.definition: Retrieval-augmented system cites incorrect or non-existent source
C09.example: Customer support bot cited non-existent policy clause

C10.name: Confident Confabulation
C10.definition: Model asserts fabricated facts with high apparent confidence
C10.example: Medical assistant stated incorrect drug dosage without uncertainty

C11.name: Context Boundary Violation
C11.definition: Model uses knowledge beyond provided context inappropriately
C11.example: Document QA system answered from training data not the uploaded doc

C12.name: Temporal Hallucination
C12.definition: Model generates outdated or time-inconsistent information
C12.example: System cited regulations superseded two years prior

C13.name: Audit Trail Deficiency
C13.definition: Insufficient logging to reconstruct model decision path
C13.example: Could not determine which prompt version caused incident

C14.name: User Trust Erosion
C14.definition: User confidence in system decreases due to opaque outputs
C14.example: Users stopped using recommendations they could not verify

C15.name: Regulatory Explanation Gap
C15.definition: System cannot produce explanations meeting regulatory requirements
C15.example: EU AI Act high-risk classification requires explainability not implemented

C16.name: Developer Interpretability Need
C16.definition: Engineering team cannot diagnose failure without explanation
C16.example: Root cause analysis required manual inspection of 10K logs

C17.name: Provider Dependency Risk
C17.definition: Over-reliance on single LLM provider creates availability risk
C17.example: GPT-4 outage caused complete product unavailability for 4 hours

C18.name: Latency SLA Breach
C18.definition: Response latency exceeds user experience threshold
C18.example: P95 latency spikes to 8s during peak load; threshold is 2s

C19.name: Cost Quality Decoupling
C19.definition: API cost increases without corresponding quality improvement
C19.example: Moved to GPT-4 at 10x cost; quality improvement not measurable

C20.name: Integration Testing Gap
C20.definition: LLM integration not tested as component boundary
C20.example: Unit tests pass; integration fails due to context window truncation

C21.name: Domain Threshold Divergence
C21.definition: Industry-specific acceptable quality differs from generic defaults
C21.example: Medical team required 99%+ factual precision vs 85% default

C22.name: Threshold Instability
C22.definition: Acceptable quality thresholds change as system matures or regulations evolve
C22.example: EU AI Act passage required immediate threshold review

C23.name: Missing Baseline Reference
C23.definition: No established industry baseline for LLM quality metrics
C23.example: Could not determine if 0.87 faithfulness score is good or bad

C24.name: Implicit Prompt Regression
C24.definition: Quality degradation from minor prompt wording changes
C24.example: Changing 'respond concisely' to 'be brief' increased hallucination rate

C25.name: Prompt Version Control Absence
C25.definition: No systematic tracking of prompt template changes
C25.example: Team had no record of which prompt version was live during incident

C26.name: Cross-System Prompt Instability
C26.definition: Prompts tuned for one model degrade on another
C26.example: GPT-4 optimised prompt failed on Claude deployment

C27.name: HIPAA Compliance Pressure
C27.definition: Healthcare data privacy requirements impose strict quality constraints
C27.example: PHI in model output triggered mandatory breach notification

C28.name: EU AI Act Uncertainty
C28.definition: Evolving AI regulation creates compliance ambiguity
C28.example: High-risk classification unclear; team implemented precautionary measures

C29.name: Audit Evidence Gap
C29.definition: Cannot produce machine-readable audit evidence for regulators
C29.example: Financial regulator requested model decision logs; none existed

C30.name: Multi-Tool Monitoring Burden
C30.definition: Quality monitoring requires integrating many disparate tools
C30.example: Team used Prometheus, Datadog, custom NLI scripts, and spreadsheets
